questions.text: If a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil so that maximum lines of force passes through the coil, the galvanometer shows:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil in such a way that maximum magnetic field lines pass through the coil, the galvanometer will show no deflection, hence this option is incorrect.
If a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil in such a way that maximum magnetic field lines pass through the coil, the galvanometer will show no deflection, hence this option is incorrect.

The galvanometer will show no current when a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil so that the maximum lines of magnetic force pass through the coil.When the magnet is stationary and its magnetic field is not changing, there will be no induced electromotive force (EMF) and therefore no induced current in the coil. The galvanometer will show no current.For induced current to flow, there must be a changing magnetic field. If you were to move the magnet relative to the coil, such as by sliding it back and forth, or rotating it, the magnetic field passing through the coil would change, and this changing magnetic field would induce an EMF and a corresponding induced current in the coil. The magnitude of the induced current would depend on factors such as the rate of change of the magnetic field and the properties of the coil.
If a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil in such a way that maximum magnetic field lines pass through the coil, the galvanometer will show no deflection. Hence, this is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The galvanometer will show no current when a stationary bar magnet is placed near a coil so that the maximum lines of magnetic force pass through the coil.When the magnet is stationary and its magnetic field is not changing, there will be no induced electromotive force (EMF) and therefore no induced current in the coil. The galvanometer will show no current.For induced current to flow, there must be a changing magnetic field. If you were to move the magnet relative to the coil, such as by sliding it back and forth, or rotating it, the magnetic field passing through the coil would change, and this changing magnetic field would induce an EMF and a corresponding induced current in the coil. The magnitude of the induced current would depend on factors such as the rate of change of the magnetic field and the properties of the coil.

questions.text: The current passing through the coil of a galvanometer is directly proportional to the:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a galvanometer, the current passing through the coil is directly proportional to the angle of deflection of the pointer or needle. The deflection of the pointer is caused by the current flowing through the coil, and the scale on the galvanometer is calibrated to indicate the current based on the angle of deflection. So, as the current passing through the coil increases, the angle of deflection of the pointer also increases, and the relationship is directly proportional. This is a fundamental principle of galvanometers, which are often used as the basis for ammeters to measure current.

questions.text: A galvanometer can be converted into a voltmeter by connecting it with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter, you need to connect a resistor in series with it. The resistor is used to limit the current passing through the galvanometer when it's connected across a voltage source, which would otherwise damage the galvanometer. The resistance of the resistor should be relatively high so that it doesn't draw too much current and affect the voltage being measured.

To convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter, you need to connect a resistor in series with it. The resistor is used to limit the current passing through the galvanometer when it's connected across a voltage source, which would otherwise damage the galvanometer. The resistance of the resistor should be relatively high so that it doesn't draw too much current and affect the voltage being measured.

The resistor is used to limit the current passing through the galvanometer when it's connected across a voltage source, which would otherwise damage the galvanometer. The resistance of the resistor should be relatively high so that it doesn't draw too much current and affect the voltage being measured.

The resistor is used to limit the current passing through the galvanometer when it's connected across a voltage source, which would otherwise damage the galvanometer. The resistance of the resistor should be relatively high so that it doesn't draw too much current and affect the voltage being measured.

questions.explanation: To convert a galvanometer into a voltmeter, you need to connect a resistor in series with it. The resistor is used to limit the current passing through the galvanometer when it's connected across a voltage source, which would otherwise damage the galvanometer. The resistance of the resistor should be relatively high so that it doesn't draw too much current and affect the voltage being measured.So, when you connect a high resistance in series with a galvanometer, it becomes a voltmeter. This added resistance forms a voltage divider with the internal resistance of the galvanometer, and the voltmeter can measure the voltage across its terminals without drawing excessive current.

questions.text: Which one of the following quantities is not measured by avometer?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An ammeter or multimeter does not directly measure electric charge. It primarily measures current (in amperes), potential difference (in volts), and resistance (in ohms). Electric charge is a fundamental quantity related to the flow of electrons, and it is not directly measured by these devices.
An ammeter, as the name suggests, is used to measure electric current. It provides a reading of the current flowing in a circuit in amperes (A).
An ammeter or multimeter can measure electrical resistance. This is useful for checking the resistance of components or wires in a circuit and is measured in ohms (Ω).

A multimeter can measure the potential difference (voltage) in a circuit. It provides readings in volts (V) and is commonly used to measure voltage across components or between points in an electrical circuit.

questions.explanation: An avometer (multimeter) can measure current, resistance, and potential difference, but it does not directly measure electric charge. Charge measurements usually require other specialized instruments or methods.

questions.text: The working principle of a galvanometer is based upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option does not accurately describe the working principle of a galvanometer, which is fundamentally based on torque and the interaction between an electric current and a magnetic field.

A galvanometer is an instrument used for detecting and measuring electric current. Its working principle is based on the concept of torque. When an electric current flows through a coil of wire within a magnetic field, it experiences a torque, which causes the coil to rotate. This rotation is proportional to the strength of the current passing through the coil. The torque on the coil in a galvanometer is generated by the interaction between the magnetic field and the magnetic moment of the coil, which is induced by the current passing through it. The greater the current, the greater the torque, leading to a larger deflection of the galvanometer needle.
This option does not accurately describe the working principle of a galvanometer, which is fundamentally based on torque and the interaction between an electric current and a magnetic field.
This option does not accurately describe the working principle of a galvanometer, which is fundamentally based on torque and the interaction between an electric current and a magnetic field.

questions.explanation: Option B) Torque is corrrect. A galvanometer is an instrument used for detecting and measuring electric current. Its working principle is based on the concept of torque. When an electric current flows through a coil of wire within a magnetic field, it experiences a torque, which causes the coil to rotate. This rotation is proportional to the strength of the current passing through the coil. The torque on the coil in a galvanometer is generated by the interaction between the magnetic field and the magnetic moment of the coil, which is induced by the current passing through it. The greater the current, the greater the torque, leading to a larger deflection of the galvanometer needle.

questions.text: A shunted galvanometer is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.

A shunted galvanometer is typically referred to as an ammeter. An ammeter is an instrument used to measure electric current in a circuit. It is connected in parallel with the circuit (shunted) so that it has a very low resistance to minimize the impact on the circuit's operation. This low resistance allows it to measure the current flowing through the circuit without significantly affecting that current. By shunting a galvanometer with a low-resistance shunt, it can be effectively converted into an ammeter to measure higher currents while maintaining good accuracy.
This option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: A shunted galvanometer is typically referred to as an ammeter. An ammeter is an instrument used to measure electric current in a circuit. It is connected in parallel with the circuit (shunted) so that it has a very low resistance to minimize the impact on the circuit's operation. This low resistance allows it to measure the current flowing through the circuit without significantly affecting that current. By shunting a galvanometer with a low-resistance shunt, it can be effectively converted into an ammeter to measure higher currents while maintaining good accuracy.

questions.text: The combination of ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter into a compact single meter with one common galvanometer is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A post-office box is unrelated to the combination of electrical meters. It is simply a rented mailbox at a post office for receiving mail.
An AVO meter, or multimeter, integrates an ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter into one device, using a common galvanometer to measure current, voltage, and resistance.
A meter bridge is a tool used to measure electrical resistance using the Wheatstone bridge principle, not a combination of ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter.
A microscope magnifies small objects for detailed observation, unrelated to measuring electrical quantities or combining different meters.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the AVO meter, also known as a multimeter. It combines an ammeter, voltmeter, and ohmmeter into one device using a common galvanometer, allowing it to measure current (both AC and DC), voltage, and resistance. Other options are incorrect because a post office box is a mail-related item, a meter bridge is used for measuring resistance specifically via the Wheatstone bridge method, and a microscope is an optical device for observing small objects.

questions.text: What shunt resistance must be connected across a Galvanometer of 20 Ω resistance which gives full scale deflection with 2.0 A current, so as to convert it into an Ammeter of range 10 A?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The shunt resistance is calculated using the formula Rs = G / (n - 1), where n = I1 / I2. Substituting the given values, we find that Rs = 5 Ω.
This is incorrect. Using a shunt resistance of 2 Ω would not provide the desired ammeter range of 10 A. Double-check your calculations using the correct formula.
This is incorrect. A shunt resistance of 3 Ω is too low to achieve the required range conversion. Ensure you are using the correct formula and values.
This is incorrect. Using a shunt resistance of 4 Ω would not convert the galvanometer to the desired range of 10 A. Verify your calculations.

questions.explanation: Explanation: Galvanometer resistance = G = 20Ω Current = I1 = 10A Current = I2 = 2 A Shunt resistance = Rs = ?So, n = I1 / I2 n = 10 / 2 = 5Applying the formula: Rs = G / n - 1 = 20 / 5 - 1 = 20 / 4 Rs = 5 ohms

questions.text: We have two coils placed close to each other. When we switch on the battery connected to primary coil while keeping the sliding contact of rheostat at fixed position, the reading of Galvanometer:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: First increases and then becomes zero
options.explanation: At the moment the switch is closed, a changing magnetic field is created. This induces a current in the secondary coil, causing the galvanometer to show a deflection. As the magnetic field stabilizes, the induced current ceases, and the galvanometer reading returns to zero.

options.text: First increases and then becomes constant at some value
options.explanation: This is incorrect because the induced current in the secondary coil is only temporary, occurring during the change in magnetic flux. Once the magnetic field stabilizes, there is no further change in flux, so the current and galvanometer reading cannot remain constant.

options.text: Increases with the passage of time
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the induced current is a result of the change in magnetic flux, which is only momentary when the switch is closed. The current does not increase continuously over time.
This option is incorrect because the initial change in magnetic flux does induce a short-lived current in the secondary coil, resulting in a momentary deflection of the galvanometer.

questions.explanation: Explanation:At that instant when the switch is closed momentarily current flows through the secondary coil due to change in magnetic flux. Galvanometer shows deflection after that the further flux will change so current in secondary will be zero. This option is not correct because the current induced in the secondary coil is not sustained, and therefore, the galvanometer reading cannot remain constant. This option is also incorrect because the current induced in the secondary coil decays with time due to the resistance of the circuit, and therefore, the galvanometer reading cannot increase with time. This option is also incorrect because a momentary current is induced in the secondary coil due to mutual induction, and this current causes a momentary deflection in the galvanometer. Therefore, the galvanometer reading cannot remain zero.

questions.text: The sensitivity of a galvanometer can be decreased by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. Increasing the magnetic field enhances the torque on the coil, thereby increasing the sensitivity of the galvanometer.

options.text: Increasing the number of turns of the coil
options.explanation: Incorrect. Adding more turns amplifies the deflection for a given current, which increases the sensitivity.
Correct. Increasing the c/BAN ratio reduces the deflection per unit current, decreasing the sensitivity.
Incorrect. Reducing the length of the couple increases the sensitivity as it reduces the counteracting torque.

questions.explanation: Sensitivity of a galvanometer is defined as the deflection per unit current. Increasing the magnetic field or the number of turns in the coil increases the sensitivity, as both enhance the torque that causes deflection. Decreasing the length of the couple 'c' also increases sensitivity by reducing the counteracting torque. However, increasing the c/BAN ratio, where 'c' is a constant related to the coil’s resistance, 'B' is the magnetic field, 'A' is the area of the coil, and 'N' is the number of turns, results in a decreased sensitivity by reducing the deflection per unit current.

questions.text: A current of 3 Amp is passing through a galvanometer. If a high resistance is connected in series to it, the total current that will pass through the resistance will be?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect. Adding resistance in a series circuit would decrease the total current, not increase it. So, the current cannot be more than the original 3 Amp.
Correct. When you add a high resistance in series, the total current in the circuit decreases. This is the only option that reflects a reduced current from the original 3 Amp.
Incorrect. While the current is the same through all components in a series circuit, the addition of a high resistance would decrease the total current from the original 3 Amp.
Incorrect. Adding resistance to the circuit would decrease the current, not increase it. Thus, 4 Amp is not a possible outcome.

questions.explanation: Current is always the same in the series circuit but attaching new resistance in a circuit would decrease current overall.By how much? This is for the amount of resistance to decide.Only option B has decreased current relative original current.

questions.text: A 0.01A moving coil Galvanometer of 5 Ω resistance can be converted into a 0.2A ammeter by a resistance R with the Galvanometer when r is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A galvanometer is converted into an ammeter by connecting a low resistance in parallel with the galvanometer. This low resistance is called shunt resistance. The formula for calculating shunt resistance isIR = ir0.01 x 5 = 0.2rr = 0.25A
This option is incorrect as they do not appreciate that shunt resistance needs to be in parallel, not series.
'C' is incorrect as it does not give the correct value of shunt resistance.
This option is incorrect as they do not appreciate that shunt resistance needs to be in parallel, not series.

questions.text: What will be the value of shunt resistance that must be connected across a galvanometer of 50.0 Ω resistance which gives full-scale deflection with 2.0 mA current so as to convert it into an ammeter of range 10.0 A?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is too high. It would not allow enough current through the shunt to achieve a 10 A range.
This value is incorrect because it does not provide the necessary low resistance path for the additional current.
While closer, this resistance is still too high to convert the galvanometer to the specified ammeter range.
This is the correct resistance needed to shunt most of the current around the galvanometer to achieve a full-scale deflection of 10 A.
This resistance is still too high to allow the galvanometer to measure up to 10 A.

questions.explanation: The correct shunt resistance is 0.01 Ω. To determine this, first calculate the voltage across the galvanometer when it shows full-scale deflection: V = I x R = 2 mA x 50 Ω = 0.1 V. To convert this galvanometer into an ammeter with a 10 A range, the total resistance must allow for 10 A to create the same 0.1 V across the galvanometer, which implies R = V/I = 0.1 V / 10 A = 0.01 Ω. This means the shunt resistor needs to provide a very low resistance path to allow most of the current to bypass the galvanometer. Therefore, the correct answer is 0.01 Ω. Other options are incorrect because they would not permit the galvanometer to measure the larger current accurately.

questions.text: Galvanometer works on the principle of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Magnetic torque refers to the torque exerted on a current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field, causing it to rotate. This is the principle on which a galvanometer operates, as it measures current by deflecting a needle proportionally to the current flow.

Ohm's Law relates voltage, current, and resistance in an electrical circuit, expressed as V = IR. It does not involve the principles of torque or magnetic fields, making it unrelated to how a galvanometer functions.

Pascal's Law deals with pressure applied to a confined fluid and is the basis for hydraulic systems. It does not involve electricity or magnetism, thus irrelevant to the working principle of a galvanometer.
Boyle's Law describes the inverse relationship between the pressure and volume of a gas at constant temperature. This is unrelated to the electromagnetic principles involved in a galvanometer.
Coulomb's Law quantifies the electric force between two charged objects. While it pertains to electrostatics, it does not explain the operation of a galvanometer, which is based on magnetic torque.

questions.explanation: A galvanometer operates on the principle of magnetic torque. When a current flows through a coil situated in a magnetic field, the interaction between the magnetic field and the current results in a torque that causes the coil to rotate. The deflection of the coil is directly proportional to the current, thus allowing the measurement of current intensity. This differs from Ohm’s Law, which relates voltage and current in a circuit, and from Pascal’s, Boyle’s, and Coulomb’s Laws, which deal with fluid mechanics, gas laws, and electrostatic forces, respectively, none of which involve the magnetic principles utilized in a galvanometer.

questions.text: A galvanometer has a resistance of 50 Ohms and gives full scale deflection when a current of 0.005 amperes flows in it. Find the value of the shunt resistance to convert it into an ammeter of range 20 amperes.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option assumes an incorrect calculation of the shunt resistance formula, possibly by misplacing the values in the formula.
Correct! The shunt resistance is calculated using the formula Rs = IgRg / (I-Ig). Substituting the values: Rs = (0.005 x 50)/(20-0.005) = 0.0125 ohms.
This option results from an incorrect application of the formula or arithmetic error during calculation.
This value is significantly higher than the calculated shunt resistance, indicating a misunderstanding of the problem.
This is an inaccurate value likely derived from incorrect calculations or assumptions.

questions.explanation: To convert a galvanometer into an ammeter, a shunt resistance is added in parallel. The formula for shunt resistance (Rs) is given by Rs = IgRg / (I-Ig), where Ig is the current for full-scale deflection of the galvanometer, Rg is the resistance of the galvanometer, and I is the total current range of the ammeter. Substituting the given values: Rs = (0.005 x 50)/(20-0.005) = 0.0125 ohms, which is Option B.Other options result from errors such as incorrect formula application, arithmetic mistakes, or misunderstanding the problem's requirements.

questions.text: A thermocouple is connected cross a galvanometer of resistance 30Ω. One junction is immersed in water at 373 K and the other in ice at 273K. The e.m.f of the thermocouple is 90μ V for each 1K difference in temperature between the junctions, and the thermocouple resistance is 6Ω. What current will flow in the galvanometer?

Hence total resistance is 36ΩSo current through galvanometer I=V/RI=9000/36=250μA

questions.text: To make an ammeter, you need a galvanometer and a resistor, specifically, you should
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Not use the resistor at all
options.explanation: (A) not use resistor at all: Not using a resistor at all would mean that all of the current would flow through the galvanometer coil, which could damage it.

options.text: Place a high resistance in series with the galvanometer coil
options.explanation: (B) place a higher resistance in series with the a galvanometer coil: Placing a higher resistance in series with the galvanometer coil would reduce the amount of current flowing through the galvanometer coil, but it would also reduce the sensitivity of the ammeter.

options.text: Place a low resistance in series with the galvanometer coil
options.explanation: (C) place a low resistance in series with the Galvanometer coil: Placing a lower resistance in series with the galvanometer coil would increase the amount of current flowing through the galvanometer coil, which could damage it.

options.text: Place a low resistance in parallel with the galvanometer coil
Place a low resistance in parallel with the galvanometer coil
options.explanation: The resistor is called a shunt resistor. The shunt resistor should have a low resistance so that most of the current flows through it. The value of the shunt resistor is chosen such that the ammeter has the desired full-scale range. For example, if you want to make an ammeter with a full-scale range of 10 amps, you would choose a shunt resistor with a resistance of 0.1 ohms.

questions.explanation: The resistor is called a shunt resistor. The shunt resistor should have a low resistance so that most of the current flows through it. The value of the shunt resistor is chosen such that the ammeter has the desired full-scale range. For example, if you want to make an ammeter with a full-scale range of 10 amps, you would choose a shunt resistor with a resistance of 0.1 ohms.

questions.text: Motional EMF can be produced with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If we change the magnetic field in space, it becomes statically induced emf because the conductor is not moving. Hence, this option is incorrect
If we change the magnetic field with time, it becomes an example of statically induced emf because the conductor is stationary. Hence, this option is also incorrect
If we change magnetic flux through space, it means the flux is variable; hence, it becomes statically induced emf because the conductor is still stationary. Hence, this option is incorrect

If the magnetic field is constant, it means that the area of the conductor must change in order to generate emf. We change the area of the conductor by moving the conductor. Hence, the emf induced will be dynamically induced emf or motional emf. Hence, this option is the correct one.

questions.explanation: A motional EMF is the EMF induced by a conductor moving through a constant magnetic field.

questions.text: For the solenoids shown in the diagram (which are assumed to be close to each other), the resistance of the left-hand circuit is slowly increased. In which direction does the current flow through the galvanometer in the right-hand circuit?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Increasing the resistance causes a decrease in the current on the left. This reduces the field strength for the solenoid B=µ0nl. By the right-hand rule, the field through the solenoid is directed to the RIGHT. Hence, in the right-hand circuit, since there will be fewer field lines directed to the right, there is an induced electric field that will produce a current in the wires to "replace" the lost field lines. This means that the current will be directed with the same orientation as the current in the left circuit and the galvanometer will deflect to the LEFT.
It will be correct only in case if the resistance of the circuit is slowly decreasing.
In this scenario current is present.So this option is incorrect.
This option is incorrect as data is sufficient to determine answer.

questions.explanation: Increasing the resistance causes a decrease in the current on the left. This reduces the field strength for the solenoid B=µ0nl. By the right-hand rule, the field through the solenoid is directed to the RIGHT. Hence, in the right-hand circuit, since there will be fewer field lines directed to the right, there is an induced electric field that will produce a current in the wires to "replace" the lost field lines. This means that the current will be directed with the same orientation as the current in the left circuit and the galvanometer will deflect to the LEFT.

questions.text: Current can be induced in a coil by changing the area of coil placed in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a uniform magnetic field, changing the area of the coil can lead to a change in magnetic flux, which is necessary for inducing an electromotive force (EMF) and consequently a current.
While both fields are present, the electric field does not contribute to the induction of current due to a change in area of the coil. Induction relies primarily on changes in magnetic flux.
A uniform electric field does not induce current in a coil through changing the area; induction requires a magnetic field to create a change in magnetic flux.
Not all scenarios lead to induced current through changing the area of the coil. Only a uniform magnetic field is necessary for inducing current in this context.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Uniform magnetic field. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in a coil when there is a change in magnetic flux through the coil. Changing the area of the coil within a uniform magnetic field alters the magnetic flux, thereby inducing a current.Options involving uniform electric fields are incorrect because electric fields do not contribute to the induction of current in this scenario. Furthermore, having both electric and magnetic fields present does not guarantee induced current, as the key factor is the change in magnetic flux. The option 'All of these' is also incorrect because only a uniform magnetic field is sufficient for the induction of current in the context of changing the area of the coil.

questions.text: The figure shows a bar moving to the right on two conducting rails. To make an induced current 'I' in the direction indicated, a constant magnetic field in the region "A" should be in what direction?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Applying Fleming's left-hand rule (LHR), the direction should be into the page.
Applying Fleming's left-hand rule (LHR), the direction should be into the page.

Applying Fleming's left-hand rule (LHR), we point our thumb in the direction of v and our middle finger in the direction of the current. In this case, our index finger (representing field) points out of the page. Since this tells us the direction of the conventional current, the reverse (into the page) will give us the direction of the electric current.
Applying Fleming's left-hand rule (LHR), the direction should be into the page.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below:

questions.text: A magnet is introduced into the coil and a voltage is induced across the coil. Which of the following factor has no effect on the induced votage?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Thickness of the wire of the coil
options.explanation: The thickness (diameter) of the wire in a coil can have an effect on the induced voltage, although it might not be a primary factor. The induced voltage in a coil is mainly determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors like the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnetic field change, the number of turns in the coil, and the orientation of the coil. The thickness of the wire affects the coil's resistance and self-inductance. Thicker wires generally have lower resistance, which means that the induced current due to the induced voltage might be larger in a coil with thicker wire. Additionally, the thickness of the wire could influence the coil's self-inductance, affecting how the coil reacts to changes in current. However, the thickness of the wire is not usually a primary factor in determining the induced voltage compared to factors like the rate of change of magnetic flux or the strength of the magnetic field.

options.text: The time in which magnet is introduced
options.explanation: The time in which a magnet is introduced into a coil does not affect the induced voltage. The induced voltage in a coil is determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors such as the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnet's movement, and the number of turns in the coil. The specific time at which the magnet is introduced does not directly impact the induced voltage, as long as the rate of change of magnetic flux remains the same. The induced voltage is a result of how quickly the magnetic flux changes, not the exact moment at which the magnet is introduced.

options.text: The strength of the magnetic field
options.explanation: The strength of the magnetic field does have an effect on the induced voltage in a coil. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced voltage (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil. The magnetic flux is affected by the strength of the magnetic field, among other factors. If the strength of the magnetic field increases, assuming other factors remain constant, the rate of change of magnetic flux will increase as well. This, in turn, will lead to a higher induced voltage. So, the strength of the magnetic field does indeed affect the induced voltage in a coil.

options.text: Number of turns of the coil
options.explanation: The number of turns in the coil does indeed have an effect on the induced voltage. According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced voltage (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil. The magnetic flux is influenced by factors including the strength of the magnetic field and the number of turns in the coil. Specifically, if the number of turns in the coil increases, the induced voltage will also increase for the same rate of change of magnetic flux. This is because each turn of the coil contributes to the overall flux linkage, leading to a higher induced voltage. So, the number of turns of the coil does have an effect on the induced voltage.

questions.explanation: The time in which a magnet is introduced into a coil does not affect the induced voltage. The induced voltage in a coil is determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil, which is influenced by factors such as the strength of the magnetic field, the speed of the magnet's movement, and the number of turns in the coil. The specific time at which the magnet is introduced does not directly impact the induced voltage, as long as the rate of change of magnetic flux remains the same. The induced voltage is a result of how quickly the magnetic flux changes, not the exact moment at which the magnet is introduced.

questions.text: A square loop of wire lies in the plane of the page. A decreasing magnetic field is directed into the page. The induced current in the loop is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the magnetic field were increasing into the page, the induced current would be counterclockwise due to Lenz's law. However, in this scenario, the magnetic field is decreasing into the page, leading to a clockwise induced current.

When the magnetic field decreases into the page, the induced current will flow in such a way that it creates a magnetic field opposing the decrease. This is in accordance with Lenz's law, which states that the induced current will always act to oppose the change in the magnetic field.
A zero current would mean there's no induced current, but this would not align with Lenz's law, which requires an induced current to oppose the change in the magnetic field.

options.text: Up the left edge and from right to left along the top edge
options.explanation: This option describes a specific path of current flow, which is not consistent with the expected response of a loop to a decreasing magnetic field. The induced current should circulate within the loop, not along its edges.

questions.explanation: As the square loop or wire lies in the plane of the page and decreasibg magnetic fiel is directed into the page. According to lenz law the induce current opposes the decreasing magnetic flux. By right hand rule the direction of induce current will be clockwise.

questions.text: If the emf across the conductor of length lm moving with a uniform speed at right angles to a magnetic field of 0.5T is 2V, the velocity of the conductor is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Please see the given explanation.
This option is incorrect. Please see the given explanation.
This option is correct. Please see the given explanation.
This option is incorrect. Please see the given explanation.

questions.explanation: Explanation is given below: The electromotive force (emf) induced in a conductor moving at right angles to a magnetic field can be calculated using Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction: emf = B * v * L Where: emf = Electromotive force (2V in this case) B = Magnetic field strength (0.5T) v = Velocity of the conductor (which we want to find) L = Length of the conductor (1m) Now, we can rearrange the equation to solve for the velocity (v):v = emf / (B * L) Plugging in the given values:v = 2V / (0.5T * 1m)v = 2V / 0.5Tv = 4 m/s So, the velocity of the conductor is 4 meters per second.

questions.text: Which of the following is the proper way to study the sinusoidal waveform of the voltage?

options.text: Voltage is connected to X input and the time base is switched off

questions.text: In the given figure, a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field. The direction of the magnetic field will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option implies that the magnetic field is in the same direction as the current flowing in the conductor. However, according to the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is perpendicular to the direction of the current. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

90 degrees: This is the correct option. As explained earlier, the magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the conductor, and the direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the current flow. In this case, it points towards the east (to the right) relative to the current flow.

180 degrees: This option suggests that the magnetic field is in the opposite direction to the current flow. However, according to the right-hand rule, the magnetic field is perpendicular to the current and forms circles around the conductor. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

270 degrees: This option implies that the magnetic field is perpendicular to the current flow but in the opposite direction. However, as mentioned earlier, the magnetic field forms circles around the conductor and is perpendicular to the current. Therefore, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: 90 degrees: This is the correct option. As explained earlier, the magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the conductor, and the direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the current flow. In this case, it points towards the east (to the right) relative to the current flow.

questions.text: In the given diagram, the directions of the induced currents through the coils, when the magnet moves toward the right, are
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Coil 1:Current from B → A means anticlockwise current (viewed from magnet side).That produces a north pole facing the magnet. Correct — because the magnet’s north is moving away, coil must act as north to oppose the loss of flux.▪ Coil 2:Current from C → D means anticlockwise current (same sense as coil 1).That makes coil 2’s left face (facing magnet) also a north pole. Wrong — coil 2 should act as south (since N pole is approaching and flux is increasing).

Coil 1:Current B → A = anticlockwise → north pole toward magnet ▪ Coil 2:Current D → C = clockwise → south pole toward magnet Option B is correct. Both polarities properly oppose the change in flux in accordance with Lenz’s law.

Coil 1:Current A → B = clockwise → south pole toward magnet But magnet’s N is receding, so coil should act north, not south.▪ Coil 2:Current C → D = anticlockwise → north pole toward magnet Magnet’s N is approaching, so coil 2 should act south.
Coil 1:Current A → B = clockwise → south pole toward magnet (wrong polarity)▪ Coil 2:Current D → C = clockwise → south pole toward magnet (correct for coil 2)

questions.explanation: Coil 1:Current B → A = anticlockwise → north pole toward magnet ▪ Coil 2:Current D → C = clockwise → south pole toward magnet Option B is correct. Both polarities properly oppose the change in flux in accordance with Lenz’s law.

questions.text: The Curie point shows a relationship between:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The Curie point is not directly related to pressure changes but is primarily concerned with temperature effects on magnetism. While pressure can have some influence on magnetic properties, it is not the primary factor in determining the Curie point.Hence, option A is not the correct answer.

The Curie point is unrelated to electric flux, which is a measure of the flow of an electric field through a given area. Electric flux is primarily influenced by electric field strength and the orientation of the electric field lines, not by pressure.Hence, option B is not the correct answer.

The Curie point refers to the temperature at which certain materials undergo a phase transition, causing a significant change in their magnetic properties. Specifically, it is the temperature at which a material's ferromagnetic or ferrimagnetic properties disappear. The correct answer is C: the Curie point shows a relationship between temperature and magnetism.

The Curie point is not associated with the relationship between temperature and electric flux. Electric flux is primarily influenced by electric field strength and the configuration of charges, while the Curie point specifically pertains to the magnetic properties of certain materials.Hence, option D is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The Curie point refers to the temperature at which certain materials undergo a phase transition, causing a significant change in their magnetic properties. Specifically, it is the temperature at which a material's ferromagnetic or ferrimagnetic properties disappear. The correct answer is C: the Curie point shows a relationship between temperature and magnetism.

questions.text: _ is a device which makes use of mutual induction for stepping up or down an alternating e.m.f.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: SONOMETER:The device that is used for demonstrating the relationship between the frequency of the sound that is produced by the string when it is plucked and the tension, length, and mass per unit length of the string.

A transformer is an electrical device that uses the principle of electromagnetic induction to transfer energy from one electric circuit to another. It is designed to either increase or decrease AC voltage between the circuits while maintaining the frequency of the current.
AMMETER :An ammeter is an instrument used to measure the current in a circuit.
VOLTMETER :A voltmeter is an instrument used for measuring electric potential difference between two points in an electric circuit. It is connected in parallel

POTENTIOMETER:A potentiometer is a three-terminal resistor with a sliding or rotating contact that forms an adjustable voltage divider. If only two terminals are used, one end and the wiper, it acts as a variable resistor or rheostat.

questions.text: When magnetic flux changes, an induced emf is produced, which generates electric field in the region. The strength of the electric field depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect according to the explanation given below.
This option is incorrect according to the explanation given below.

options.text: Rate of change of magnetic flux
options.explanation: The strength of the electric field induced by a changing magnetic flux depends on a couple of factors:1. Rate of change of magnetic flux: The faster the magnetic flux changes, the greater the induced electromotive force (emf) and, consequently, the stronger the electric field generated in the region.2. Area enclosed by the changing magnetic flux: A larger area enclosed by the changing magnetic flux leads to a stronger induced emf and a stronger electric field.So, the strength of the electric field induced by a changing magnetic flux is directly related to the rate of change of magnetic flux and the area through which the flux passes.
As one of the above options is correct, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The strength of the electric field induced by a changing magnetic flux depends on a couple of factors:1. Rate of change of magnetic flux: The faster the magnetic flux changes, the greater the induced electromotive force (emf) and, consequently, the stronger the electric field generated in the region.2. Area enclosed by the changing magnetic flux: A larger area enclosed by the changing magnetic flux leads to a stronger induced emf and a stronger electric field.So, the strength of the electric field induced by a changing magnetic flux is directly related to the rate of change of magnetic flux and the area through which the flux passes.

questions.text: Which law is based upon the law of conservation of energy?
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Lenz's law upholds the general principle of the conservation of energy. It states that current induced in a circuit would flow in a direction that would always oppose the change that induced the current.
